title: The Straits Impregnable
description: An Australian war novel whose publication collided with military censorship during World War I.
descriptionParagraphs: An Australian war novel whose publication collided with military censorship during World War I.

The Straits Impregnable is usually read through its treatment of war, military censorship, and politics. As a novel, it turns those concerns into conflicts of character, voice, setting, and social pressure rather than leaving them as abstract ideas.

Part of the work's durability lies in the way its form intensifies its themes. Readers return to it not only for subject matter but for the distinctive voice, structure, and atmosphere through which it makes war, military censorship, and politics feel immediate.

cover.alt: The Straits Impregnable cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: An Australian war novel whose publication collided with military censorship during World War I.
banningParagraphs: The Straits Impregnable entered censorship debates as a novel associated with war, military censorship, and politics. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around wartime censorship and national security.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1914 in Australia, where Military censor under the War Precautions Act banned publication. The second edition was suppressed by military censors during wartime. This entry is useful because the state objection was strategic and military, not moral.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
counterReadings.author: Hannah Arendt
counterReadings.note: A foundational analysis of state terror, propaganda, and ideological conformity.

counterReadings.title: On Tyranny
counterReadings.author: Timothy Snyder
counterReadings.note: A short modern guide to resisting authoritarian politics and controlled public discourse.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
